## Learning outcomes

By the end of this part, you should be able to:

- Direct `Claude Code` to scaffold and extend a `Next.js` app of your own choosing across multiple sessions
- Practise the sprint's context habits: a `CLAUDE.md` with real rules, a `docs/` folder, and the search → paste → cite pattern
- Run and verify a `Next.js` app locally in your browser

<br>

---

<br>

## Overview

This is the Sprint 1 sprint project, where every habit you have built across the sprint comes together: directing `Claude Code` with a clear brief and managing context with `CLAUDE.md` and a `docs/` folder.

The twist is that **you choose what to build.** Instead of a prescribed app, you use the techniques from this sprint to build a small `Next.js` app of your own. Pick something you would actually use. If nothing comes to mind, there is a document-app sketch further down you can build instead.

The point of this project is to practise the workflow, not to ship a polished product. **Keep it small and do not try to perfect it.** Later sprints add real databases, user accounts, deployment, and more technique, so anything you leave rough now, you will have better tools for soon.

**This project runs locally only: it is not deployed anywhere, and you are not expected to put it on the public internet.** You have not been taught how to deploy a `Next.js` app yet, and you do not need to. Everything (building, testing, and submitting) happens on your own machine at `localhost:3000`. Putting an app on a public URL is something a later sprint covers, once it has a backend and user accounts to support it.

#### Topics

- `Next.js` App Router: pages, layouts, and dynamic routes
- Choosing a persistence mechanism for a no-backend app, in consultation with `Claude Code`
- Multi-session `Claude Code` workflows and context engineering

#### Prerequisites

- Completed the static-site lesson and the version-control lesson in this sprint
- A `GitHub` account
- `Claude Code` installed and authenticated

<br>

---

<br>

## Task description

You are building a small `Next.js` app of your own choosing. It might be a recipe box, a workout log, a reading list, a link saver, or the document app sketched below. What the app *is* is up to you. How you build it, and the framework you build it with, are fixed.

The stack stays the same whatever you build:

- `Next.js` (App Router)
- `TypeScript`
- `Tailwind CSS`

A few more things are fixed, so the sprint's skills actually get practised:

- The app runs locally via `npm run dev` (the command that starts your app on your machine) and you check it at `localhost:3000`.
- There is no deployment step and no public URL.
- There is no backend and no user accounts.
- Data persists through a browser mechanism you decide on with `Claude Code` (see *Approach to solving the task* below).

Everything else is your call: what the app does, how it looks, what features it has beyond the must-haves.

You will not write any of this code yourself. Your job is to write the brief, keep the agent accurate with context and references, and own the outcome.

<br>

---

<br>

## Must-have capabilities

Whatever you build, it should show the capabilities below, so the sprint's framework skills get exercised. **Adapt each one to your chosen app**, and keep the whole thing small: one core thing done well.

- [ ] At least two pages, including one detail page with its own web address (for example `/items/abc123`) that opens the right item directly.
- [ ] The user can create, edit, and delete the main thing your app is about.
- [ ] What the user creates is still there after a full page reload.
- [ ] No blank screens: a readable message when there is nothing yet, and a friendly "not found" message for an address that does not exist.
- [ ] A usable, responsive layout that works on a laptop and on a phone.

Beyond these, add whatever fits your app. But resist the urge to over-scope.

<br>

---

<br>

## Need an idea? The document app

No idea of your own? Build the document app. It is a personal, browser-based writing workspace, and the sketch below is **one way to build it, not a checklist to satisfy.**

One way to shape it:

- A two-pane workspace: a list of documents down one side, an editing area on the other.

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/bc8c3b08-aec0-46f5-8d1b-0466711772fe.png" width="800" alt="Two-pane document workspace layout" />

- The user can create a new document, edit its title and body, and delete it.
- A search box in the list that filters documents by title as the user types.
- Documents that are still there when the page is reloaded.
- Each document at its own web address, so a bookmark or shared link opens it directly.
- Basic Markdown formatting in the body (headings, bold, italic, bullet lists), with a way to switch between editing and a formatted preview.

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/e0941581-e447-4651-8bd2-22ad712b3517.png" width="800" alt="Markdown editor with edit and preview modes" />

- A layout that stays usable from laptop width down to phone width.

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/060895a3-373d-4724-825e-811bb52104d7.png" width="800" alt="Desktop and mobile responsive layouts of the document app" />

<br>

### Other ideas

Any small, single-focus app works. A few one-liners to spark something:

- **Recipe box:** save recipes, open each at its own address, search by name.
- **Workout log:** log sessions, edit and delete them, and still see them after a reload.
- **Link saver:** paste a link with a note, tag it, and open each saved item's page.
- **Reading list:** add books, mark them read, and filter the list.

<br>

---

<br>

## Stretch ideas (optional)

These are **ideas, not requirements.** If your must-haves work and you want to practise directing the agent through a feature that was not in the original scaffold, pick one. Or invent your own.

- **Starred or pinned items.** A star button pins favourites to the top of the list, and the starred state survives a reload.
- **Dark-mode toggle.** A switch flips the app between light and dark themes, and the choice sticks across reloads.
- **Tags.** Add tags to each item, show them in the list, and let the user filter by tag.
- **Export / import.** A button that downloads everything as a file, and a picker that reads it back in.
- **Live count or stats.** A running total somewhere useful, such as a word count or an item count.

Ship one only if you have the time and the appetite.

<br>

---

<br>

## Habits worth carrying into this build

These are the habits from this sprint worth carrying into your build. They are **encouraged best practice, not pass/fail criteria.** Lean on them because they make the build smoother, and because they are exactly what you will talk through in your review.

- **A `CLAUDE.md` with real rules.** A short file at the repo root describing what the app is, the stack, how to run it, and one or two plain-language rules (for example, *"use the App Router, not the Pages Router"* or *"do not add new libraries without asking first"*).
- **A `docs/` folder with a cited reference.** At least one `Next.js` reference you pasted in, with the source URL at the top, so the agent works from real documentation rather than guesswork.
- **The persistence consultation.** Before building features, ask `Claude Code` how best to persist data for a single-user, no-backend browser app, and settle on a mechanism (see *Approach to solving the task*).
- **A sensible `.gitignore`.** Keep `node_modules/`, `.env`, and OS or editor clutter out of the repo, the same habit you rehearsed in the practice project.
- **A short design pass.** Spend a focused stretch giving the agent specific visual direction so the app looks deliberately styled.

> **Reflection:** You will not be graded on ticking every habit off. You will be asked to talk about the ones you used and what they changed.

<br>

---

<br>

## Evaluation criteria

This project is reviewed locally. There is no deployed URL and no hosted environment to check: open the app at `localhost:3000` to evaluate it.

The review centres on three things:

- **A working `Next.js` app of your own choosing** that meets the must-have capabilities and runs locally at `localhost:3000`.
- **Evidence you directed the agent using the sprint's techniques**, as things you can talk through in the review: how you kept the agent accurate (`CLAUDE.md`, a `docs/` folder, search → paste → cite).
- **The persistence decision**, if you ran the consultation: which mechanism you chose and why it fits a single-user, no-backend app.

> **Note:** A small, unpolished, incomplete-but-working app that clearly demonstrates the workflow passes. Completeness and polish are not expected at this stage, and there is no requirement to ship a stretch feature.

<br>

---

<br>

## Approach to solving the task

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/3c5c812b-011e-4f8c-953b-75191ce463bc.png" width="600" alt="Next.js App Router getting started documentation" />

*Next.js App Router getting started overview with topic navigation*

**Decide what to build and scope it down first.** Write one or two sentences describing your app and the single thing it should do well. If it needs more than a handful of screens, it is too big. Trim it, or build the document app sketched above.

Then write `CLAUDE.md` and seed a `docs/` folder with at least one cited `Next.js` reference before you scaffold the project. Start at [nextjs.org/docs](https://nextjs.org/docs): read the relevant sections, paste the content in, and add the source URL at the top of the file.

Before building any features, consult `Claude Code` on persistence. Describe your app in plain language and ask how best to keep its data around:

```text
I'm building a small single-user app that runs only in my browser on my own
machine. There's no server, no database, and no login. I need whatever the
user creates to still be there after they close the tab and come back. What
are my options for saving that data, and which one fits best? Explain the
trade-offs before you recommend one.
```

Walk out with a clear answer to two questions: which mechanism, and why it fits. Write both into `REFLECTION.md`.

Build one feature at a time and commit at every stable state:

1. Basic shell and pages
2. Creating, editing, and deleting your main item, with persistence
3. Search and empty states
4. Responsive layout and formatting
5. An optional stretch feature

Never ask the agent to build the whole app in a single prompt.

If time allows, take 30 to 60 minutes for a design pass once the must-haves work. Give `Claude Code` specific direction rather than a vague "make it nicer": pick a tone (clean and minimal, dense, playful, editorial) and call out typography, spacing, colour palette, and button shape. Iterate until it looks intentional, not like the raw scaffold.

If a session breaks the app, reset to the last good commit.

**If you get stuck:** open a fresh `Claude Code` session, share where you are, and ask it to identify what is most likely blocking you. A fresh session often spots context drift that a long one cannot see.

<br>

---

<br>

## Submission

Read an [in-depth guide about reviews here](https://doku.turingcollege.com/p/0771306d-bc1e-4b00-9153-c63adeb63a85).

#### Submission and scheduling a project review

To submit the project and allow the reviewer to view your work beforehand, use the `GitHub` repository that has been created for you. Please go through these materials to learn more about submitting projects, scheduling project reviews, and using `GitHub`:

- [Completing a Sprint, Submitting a Project and Scheduling Reviews](https://doku-v02-production.up.railway.app/p/f3dbf8bc-2dae-4230-938d-3144110ea74a)
- [Git and GitHub for Beginners](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RGOj5yH7evk)

Hand in the following before your 1-1 review call:

- **Your Turing College `GitHub` repository URL.**
- **`README.md`** covering: what you built, how to run it locally, and a screenshot of the app.
- **`REFLECTION.md`** (250–450 words) covering the points below.

Include these too if you used them (they are what most of the review conversation draws on):

- **`CLAUDE.md`** at the repo root, reflecting the rules the agent was actually asked to follow.
- **`docs/` folder** with at least one cited `Next.js` reference, source URL at the top.

In `REFLECTION.md`, cover:

1. What you chose to build and how you scoped it down.
2. The persistence decision, if you ran the consultation: which mechanism you chose and why it fits a single-user, no-backend browser app.
3. One moment a Sprint 1 technique changed the outcome: search → paste → cite, or `CLAUDE.md` catching the agent drifting.
4. The design pass, if you did one: the direction you gave and what changed from the scaffolded default.
5. One thing that was harder than the plain-`HTML` app from the static-site lesson.
6. What you would keep or change next time.
